title: Past Tense
situation: Talk about what happened — yesterday, last week, your whole life story

phrases.context: Simple past: verb root + a/i/e. 'gaya' (male), 'gayi' (female). NO helper verb needed for 'I'
'aana' (to come) becomes 'aaya/aayi'. Pattern: verb stem + aa sound + ya/yi for gender
When the verb is transitive (has an object), subject gets 'ne': hum→humne, main→maine, usne (he/she)

phrases.hindi: maine kaha tha / maine kahi thi
phrases.english: I had said it (masc. object) / I had said it (fem. object)
phrases.context: 'tha/thi' after past verb = past perfect (had done). Gender matches OBJECT with transitive verbs!
phrases.pronunciation: MAIN-ne KA-ha tha / MAIN-ne KA-hi thi

phrases.hindi: tum kahan the? / tum kahan thi?
phrases.english: Where were you? (to male) / Where were you? (to female)
phrases.context: 'tha/thi/the' alone = was/were. 'the' for male plural/tum-male, 'thi' for female. Very useful!
phrases.pronunciation: TUM ka-HAN THE? / TUM ka-HAN THI?

phrases.context: 'usne' = he/she + ne (past transitive marker). 'mujhe' = to me. 'phone karna' = to call

phrases.english: Yesterday I didn't go to office
phrases.context: 'kal' = yesterday/tomorrow (context decides). 'nahi' before verb = didn't. Simple negation!

phrases.hindi: bachpan mein hum roz khelke aate the
phrases.english: In childhood, we used to come back after playing daily
phrases.context: Past habitual: verb + te/ti + the/thi/tha. 'aate the' = used to come. Expresses old habits
phrases.pronunciation: BACH-pan mein HUM ROZ khel-KE aa-TE THE

phrases.context: 'hua' = happened (past of 'hona' = to be/happen). One of the most common past tense phrases

phrases.hindi: maine socha ki tum aa rahe ho
phrases.english: I thought that you were coming
phrases.context: 'socha' = thought (past of 'sochna'). 'ki' = that (conjunction). Combining tenses in one sentence
phrases.pronunciation: MAIN-ne SO-cha ki TUM AA ra-HE ho

grammar_notes: Simple past: verb root + a (male sg), i (female sg), e (male plural). Example: kha-ya (ate-male), kha-yi (ate-female), kha-ye (ate-plural male)
The 'ne' rule: With transitive verbs (verbs that have an object), the subject takes 'ne' — maine, tune, usne, humne, unhone. The verb then agrees with the OBJECT's gender, not the subject!
Was/were: tha (male sg), thi (female sg), the (male plural/tum), theen (female plural). 'Main tha' = I was (male), 'Main thi' = I was (female)
Past habitual (used to): verb-ta/ti/te + tha/thi/the. 'Main jaata tha' = I used to go. Same as present tense form but with tha/thi/the instead of hoon/hai/ho
Common irregular past forms: jaana→gaya, aana→aaya, karna→kiya, dena→diya, lena→liya, hona→hua
culture_notes: 'Kal' means both yesterday AND tomorrow — you figure out which from context. If ambiguous, add 'beeta kal' (past yesterday) or 'aane wala kal' (coming tomorrow)
Indians often use past tense to be polite: 'maine socha ki...' (I thought that...) instead of directly stating what you want

skill_breakdown.explanation: For intransitive verbs (go, come, sit, sleep): verb stem + '-a' (M) or '-i' (F) — agrees with subject gender. 'main aaya' = I came (male), 'main aayi' = I came (female).

more_examples.english: she got up in the morning

skill_breakdown.explanation: Used for habits or repeated actions in the past — 'used to'. Verb stem + '-ta'/'-ti' + 'tha'/'thi'/'the'. 'main school jaati thi' = I used to go to school.

more_examples.hindi: bachpan mein main bahut khelta tha
more_examples.english: as a child, I used to play a lot (male)

more_examples.hindi: wo har Sunday chai peeti thi
more_examples.english: she used to drink chai every Sunday

more_examples.hindi: papa roz shaam ko ghar aate the
more_examples.english: papa used to come home every evening

skill_breakdown.explanation: Used for ongoing action in the past. Verb stem + 'raha'/'rahi'/'rahe' + 'tha'/'thi'/'the'. 'main jaa rahi thi' = I was going. Think of it as the past form of '-raha hai'.

more_examples.hindi: jab aap aaye, main khaana banaa rahi thi
more_examples.english: when you came, I was making food

more_examples.hindi: wo phone par baat kar raha tha
more_examples.english: he was talking on the phone

practice_prompt: You are a Hindi tutor drilling past tense — past habitual (`karta tha`), simple past (`gaya/gayi`), and perfect (`kar liya`). Address the user as `aap` and stay in Hindi/Hinglish unless they ask for English. Open with: `chaliye past tense practice karte hain — bataaiye Hindi mein: 'I used to drink chai every morning'`. Give one task at a time and mix the three past types: translate-this prompts, pick-the-right-past-form (habitual vs simple vs perfect), and conjugate-this with a given subject. Focus on the three forms' distinct uses, gender agreement (`gaya/gayi/gaye` matching the subject), and perfective auxiliary choice (`liya` for self, `diya` for other, `gaya` for finality). Briefly note that transitive simple past needs `ne` — but that has its own foundation, so don't drill it here. Flag mistakes with `[[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]]`. Watch for: confusing habitual (`pita tha`) with simple past (`piya`), wrong gender (`gaya` vs `gayi`), mistakenly inserting `ne`, and wrong perfective auxiliary. Run 6-8 items, escalating from translation to free conjugation. End with `kaafi acche se kiya aapne — phir milte hain`.

theory.intro: English speakers expect one past tense — Hindi has at least three, and which one you use changes the meaning. Past habitual is for repeated actions ("I used to..."), simple past is for one-time completed actions ("I did..."), and perfect/completive is for emphasized completion ("I'm done..."). On top of that, the verb agrees with gender — and for transitive verbs in simple past, the gender that matters is the OBJECT's, not the subject's. This is where Hindi diverges most from English.

sections.heading: Three pasts, three uses
sections.body:
Hindi splits English's single past tense into at least three distinct verb shapes. Past habitual covers repeated or customary past actions — what English does with "used to" or a bare past + frequency word ("I drank chai every morning"). Simple past covers one-time completed actions — "I drank a chai". Perfect/completive emphasizes that the action was finished or carried through to its end — "I drank it all up" or "I'm done drinking".

English leans on tense + adverbs to mark these distinctions ("used to drink" vs "drank" vs "have drunk"). Hindi changes the verb shape itself. Same verb root, three different forms — and you pick based on whether the action was a habit, a one-off event, or a completed/emphasized finish.

examples.breakdown: past habitual — pina (to drink) + ta + tha = repeated action
simple past — pina → piya, with `ne` because pina is transitive; one-time event

examples.english: I drank it (all) up / I'm done drinking

callout.body: These three aren't interchangeable. "I drank chai every morning" is HABITUAL in Hindi, not simple past. Pick the form that matches the meaning, not the English tense.

sections.cutting_intro: Heads up — English's one past tense splits into three in Hindi. Each says something different. Let's break them apart.

quick_check.question: Which form would you use for "I used to play cricket as a kid"?
quick_check.explanation: Past habitual — the action was repeated/customary in the past. `main khelta tha` = I used to play. Simple past is for one-time events; perfect is for emphasized completion.

sections.heading: Past habitual: verb-stem + -ta/-ti/-te + tha/thi/the
sections.body:
The easiest past tense to form and the safest one to start with — there's no `ne` rule here. You build it just like the present habitual, but swap `hoon/hai/hain` for `tha/thi/the`. The first piece (`-ta/-ti/-te`) agrees with the subject's gender and number. The second piece (`tha/thi/the`) also agrees with the subject.

The pattern: present habitual `main karta hoon` (I do) → past habitual `main karta tha` (I used to do). For a feminine speaker: `main karti hoon` → `main karti thi`. For plural or formal `aap`: `aap karte the` (used to do). Both halves flip together.

examples.english: he used to live in Mumbai
examples.breakdown: rehna (to live) + ta + tha — habitual past, masc. subject

examples.hindi: main har subah chai pita tha
examples.english: I used to drink chai every morning
examples.breakdown: pina + ta + tha — repeated action, masc. speaker

examples.english: she used to walk to school
examples.breakdown: jaana + ti + thi — habitual, fem. subject

sections.cutting_intro: Start with this one. Past habitual is just present habitual with `tha/thi/the` instead of `hoon/hai`. No `ne`, no object tricks.

quick_check.question: How do you say "she used to study" (padhna = to study)?
quick_check.explanation: `woh padhti thi` — feminine subject takes `-ti` + `thi`. The masculine form would be `padhta tha`; the simple past with `ne` would be `usne padha`.

sections.heading: Simple past: verb root + -a / -i / -e (intransitive)
sections.body:
Simple past covers one-time completed actions. For INTRANSITIVE verbs (verbs without a direct object — go, come, sleep, sit, fall), the verb agrees with the SUBJECT's gender. The pattern is: take the verb root, add `-a` for masc. singular, `-i` for fem. singular, `-e` for masc. plural or `aap`.

A handful of common verbs are slightly irregular — `jaana` becomes `gaya` (not `jaaya`), `hona` becomes `hua`, `lena` becomes `liya`, `dena` becomes `diya`. Memorize these as a small list; everything else follows the regular pattern.

table.caption: Common intransitive verbs in the simple past

callout.body: For TRANSITIVE verbs (verbs with an object — eat, read, do, drink), simple past uses the `ne` construction and the verb agrees with the OBJECT's gender, not the subject's. That's a whole other rule — see the noun-gender chapter and the dedicated `ne` rule lesson. For now, just remember: intransitive simple past = subject-agreement, no `ne`.

sections.cutting_intro: Next — one-time events with intransitive verbs. Subject gender controls the ending. Clean and simple.

quick_check.question: How do you say "she came" (aana = to come)?
quick_check.explanation: `woh aayi` — aana is intransitive, so the verb agrees with the subject's gender. Feminine subject → `-i` ending. `usne aaya` is wrong on two counts: no `ne` with intransitives, and the gender wouldn't match either way.

sections.heading: Perfect/completive: V + le/de/jaa + auxiliary
sections.body:
Hindi has a productive way to emphasize that an action was completed or carried all the way through: stick an auxiliary verb (`lena`, `dena`, `jaana`) onto the main verb. The main verb takes a participle-like form, and the auxiliary carries the tense and agreement. The auxiliary also tints the meaning:

- `lena` (to take) — done FOR ONESELF or FOR ONE'S OWN BENEFIT. `Maine kha liya` = I ate it (for me, and I'm done).
- `dena` (to give) — done FOR SOMEONE ELSE. `Maine kar diya` = I did it (for you / handed off).
- `jaana` (to go) — done with FINALITY, sometimes with motion. `Woh so gaya` = he fell asleep (went to sleep, completed). `Main pee gaya` = I drank it down (note: `jaana` as auxiliary overrides the `ne` rule — subject stays plain).

examples.breakdown: karna + liya — completed, for self. Common as a finished-status announcement.

examples.english: he gave me the money (handed it off)
examples.breakdown: dena + diya — completion + benefit to someone else (me)

examples.english: he was tired and fell asleep
examples.breakdown: sona + gaya — completion with finality; `jaana` marks the going-to-sleep as a done event

examples.english: I ate the food (all of it / I'm done)
examples.breakdown: khaana + liya — completed action, for self. Note `ne` because khaana is transitive.

callout.body: Default to `lena` when in doubt — it's the most common compound-past auxiliary in casual speech. `Kar liya`, `kha liya`, `pee liya`, `bol diya` are everywhere in conversation. There's a full foundation chapter on compound verbs later — this is just the past-tense flavor.

sections.cutting_intro: And now the spicy one — compound past. You stack a helper verb to mark completion. The helper colors the meaning.

quick_check.question: What's the nuance difference between `maine kiya` and `maine kar liya`?
options: No difference — both mean "I did it"
`kar liya` emphasizes completion / "I'm done with it"
`kar liya` is past habitual, `kiya` is simple past
quick_check.explanation: `maine kiya` = I did it (plain simple past). `maine kar liya` adds emphasis on completion — "I've done it, it's finished, I'm done." The `liya` auxiliary marks the finish.

sections.heading: Common pitfalls
sections.body:
The single biggest mistake English speakers make is defaulting to simple past for everything. In English we say "I drank chai every morning" with simple past — but in Hindi that's HABITUAL territory: `main har subah chai pita tha`. If you say `maine har subah chai pi` it sounds like you drank chai exactly once, every morning, as a one-shot event — grammatically odd.

The second big trap is the `ne` rule. Simple past + transitive verb = subject takes `ne`, and the verb agrees with the OBJECT's gender, not the subject's. `Maine roti khayi` (fem. object → khayi), `maine khaana khaya` (masc. object → khaya). Get this wrong and the sentence still parses, but every native speaker will hear the error. The `ne` rule has its own dedicated foundation chapter — drill it there.

Third trap: don't confuse the past-habitual `tha/thi/the` (which pairs with `-ta/-ti/-te`) with the plain `was/were` `tha/thi/the` (which stands alone). `Main thakta tha` = I used to get tired. `Main thaka tha` = I was tired (a state, not a habit). Tiny difference, big meaning change.

callout.body: When in doubt, ask: "Was this a one-time event or a repeated habit?" → habitual for repeated, simple past for one-time. THEN, if it's one-time AND transitive (has an object you're acting on), add `ne` to the subject and agree the verb with the object. Two decisions, in order.

sections.cutting_intro: One more thing — the traps. English speakers stumble on the same spots every time. Watch for these.

quick_check.question: "I drank chai every morning at the chai stall." Which Hindi form fits?
options: maine har subah chai stall pe chai pi
main har subah chai stall pe chai pita tha
maine har subah chai stall pe chai pee li
quick_check.explanation: Habitual — the phrase "every morning" signals a repeated action. `main ... chai pita tha` = I used to drink chai. The simple-past version sounds like a one-off event despite the "every morning", which is grammatically odd.

theory.wrap_up: Past tense is where Hindi gets its reputation as "hard". The three forms have distinct meanings, and the gender + `ne` agreement adds complexity that English doesn't prepare you for. The good news: it's learnable in layers. Drill the past habitual first — no `ne`, just subject-agreement. Then simple past for intransitive verbs — still just subject-agreement, no `ne`. Then graduate to the `ne` construction for transitive simple past (its own chapter). Finally, layer on the compound-verb completive forms (`kar liya`, `de diya`) as you hear them in conversation. The phrases below mix all three flavors so you can start spotting the patterns in context.
